paper_title: Public–Private Dialogs to Spur Export-led Growth: The Case of Productivity Taskforces in Namibia
research_answer: Namibia's two main high-value fruit exports have had quite different trajectories. Grapes, which represent about 70% of Namibia's fruit production, have been exported since 2000 and grew continuously until 2014, after which they declined. However, the sector has recently rebounded strongly: grape exports grew 40% between 2020 and 2022, and then a further 21% in 2023 alone. Harvested area also expanded significantly, up 28% (about 2,000 hectares) between 2018 and 2022. Dates are a much newer and smaller export story — producers only began exporting significant volumes in 2014 — but they achieved a fourfold increase in exports by 2019, and harvested area more than tripled between 2017 and 2022. Together, these trends suggest the sector is in a phase of renewed momentum after earlier setbacks, with grapes driving volume and dates showing rapid growth from a low base.
supporting_quotes: According to FAO production estimates, table grapes were 70% of fruit production in 2020 in Namibia. Grape growers have constantly increased their exports since they started in 2000, though they have declined since 2014.
Between 2018 and 2022, grape farms registered an increase of two thousand hectares or 28% in harvested area. Gross exports increased by 40% between 2020 and 2022.
In 2023, gross exports of grapes increased by 21% versus the previous year.
On the other hand, date producers started exporting significant amounts of produce in 2014 and had experienced a fourfold increase in exports by 2019.
Similarly, the harvested area for dates – although at a much lower scale – more than tripled between 2017 and 2022.
